---
title: "Analyzing Winners of Nathan's Famous Hotdog Eating Competition through Data Storytelling"
author:
  - name: Keelin Joyce
    email: kjoyc22@stlawu.edu
    affiliation:
      - id: slu
        name: St. Lawrence University
date: "February 17, 2026"
description: "A tutorial on the Closeread Quarto extension for storytelling using Nathan's Hotdog Eating Contest Winners"
editor: 
  canonical: true
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
image: sr_map.png
categories:
  - Data Visualization
  - Closeread Extention
  - Data Storytelling
---

Please note that these material have not yet completed the required
pedagogical and industry peer-reviews to become a published module on
the SCORE Network. However, instructors are still welcome to use these
materials if they are so inclined.

## Introduction

Nathan's Famous Hotdog Eating Contest is a contest held every year on
the 4th of July in which competitive eaters compete to eat the most
amount of hotdogs in a specified amount of time. Recent competitions
have been run for a total of either 10 or 12 minutes, but earlier
competitions were much shorter.

This dataset looks at the winner of the competition each year, the
gender (only some years have data on female winners), the year the
competition took place, the total runtime of the competition that year,
how many hotdogs were eaten by the winner, and variable of how many
hotdogs per minute the winner ate to account for different competition
runtimes.

![2025 Winners- Joey Chestnut and Miki
Sudo](https://www.tastingtable.com/img/gallery/everything-to-know-about-nathans-famous-july-4th-international-hot-dog-eating-contest-2024/intro-1719859047.jpg){#fig-intro}

In this module, we will look at how to use the Closeread Quarto
extension to tell a story using data.

##### Here is an example [Nathan's Hotdog Eating Contest Closeread](file:///Users/keelinjoyce/Desktop/SYE/github%20repos/stat450-spr2026-score/keelinjoyce/hotdogstory/ClosereadSolution.html){target="_blank"} telling the story of the contest using data visualization. This is what we will be creating in this module.

The data for this module comes from
[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nathan%27s_Hot_Dog_Eating_Contest){target="_blank"},
and has been cleaned into a .csv file for convenience.

Take a look at the [Nathan's Hotdog Eating Contest Wikipedia
page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nathan%27s_Hot_Dog_Eating_Contest){target="_blank"}
to see the original data and familiarize yourself with the story. There
are many anecdotes that explain changes in the data and you will want to
have an idea of these in order to effectively communicate your
storytelling.

The cleaned data set can be downloaded and imported here. 

Download Data:
[HotdogData.csv](HotdogData.csv)

```{r message=FALSE}
library(readr)
hotdog_data <- read_csv("~/Downloads/HotdogData.csv")
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Learning Objectives" appearance="minimal"}
By the end of this tutorial, students will be able to:

1.  Use data to create appropriate visualizations.

2.  Analyze these visualizations to tell a story.

3.  Use the Closeread Quarto extention to assemble data, visualizations,
    and analyses into a cohesive and visually appealing project.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Activity Length" appearance="minimal"}
This could be suitable of an out of class activity or one that spans one
or more class periods.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Methods" appearance="minimal"}
**Technology Requirement:**

This activity is designed to be completed primarily in `R` using a
Quarto document workflow with a Closeread format. Students should be
comfortable importing data and performing basic data visualizations with
packages from the tidyverse (especially `readr`, `dplyr`, and `tidyr`).

We will use the Closeread extension to assemble data storytelling. No
previous experience using Closeread is required.

</details>
:::

## Installing Closeread

Installing Closeread is simple once you have Quarto downloaded. Follow
along here or on the [Closeread website home page](https://closeread.dev)
for more information on installation and getting started.

In the directory that you want, paste this into your terminal:

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
quarto add qmd-lab/closeread
```

Anytime you are creating a Closeread document, make sure your header includes:

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
format:
  closeread-html:
```

## Getting Started

#### Learning Closeread

Before starting to create your document, look at the [Closeread
website](https://closeread.dev){target="_blank"} to get an idea of how
it works and how it can be used. There are many example codes that can
be used to create your own Closeread document. Make sure to look at the
[Gallery](https://closeread.dev/gallery/){target="_blank"} and
[Reference](https://closeread.dev/reference/){target="_blank"} pages.
There is also a lot of information at
[gastonsanchez.com](https://www.gastonsanchez.com/learn-closeread/modules/){target="_blank"}.
Get an idea of some of the basic syntax before moving forward.

#### Data

The `cleaned hotdog` dataset contains 69 rows, one per competition
champion, and includes variables of the year they won, gender, how many
hotdogs were eaten by the winner, the run time of the competition that
year, and a proportion variable of how many hotdogs were eaten per
minute.

Download Data: [HotdogData.csv](HotdogData.csv)

| Variable | Description |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Year | The year the competition took place |
| Sex | Indicator variable of sex (1 for male, 0 for female) |
| Dogs | How many total hotdogs were eaten by the winner |
| Winner | Name of the competition winner |
| Time | Time (in minutes) of the competition (not consistant year to year) |
| Hotdogs_per_minute | Proportion of how many hotdogs per minute were eaten (to account for time difference) |

#### Data Source

-   [wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nathan%27s_Hot_Dog_Eating_Contest){target="_blank"}

## Creating Your Closeread

#### Motivating question

In this module, we investigate the following research question:

> **What are some factors that contribute to how many hotdogs the winner
> of Nathan's Famous Hotdog Eating Contest eats and how can we tell that
> story using data?**

> In other words, how can we use Closeread for effective data
> storytelling?

#### Prepping Workspace

In a new Quarto Document, paste the following header to create a
Closeread document

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
---
title: Nathan's Hotdog Eating Contest
author: Your Name
format:
  closeread-html:
    cr-style:
      narrative-background-color-sidebar: "#182"
      narrative-text-color-sidebar: "#fff"
      section-background-color: "#f7f6f5"
---
```

The format for a Closeread header is similar to that of the typical
.html document with a few alterations. Most of the aesthetics are coded
for in the header.

-   `narrative-background-color-sidebar` changes the color of the
    sidebar

-   `narrative-text-color-sidebar` changes the color of the text on the
    sidebar

-   `section-background-color` changes the overall background color

There are many more aesthetic changes that can be made to the Closeread
document. Look at the [Closeread
website](https://closeread.dev){target="_blank"} to find more aesthetic
options. Feel free to style it to your preference.

To begin, we will need to load the packages we will be using. For this
module, you'll need to load the `tidyverse` and `ggrepel` packages.

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}

# If you need to install either of these uncomment the line below
# install.packages(c("tidyverse","ggrepel"))

library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
```

Now we should read in the data. Save the .csv file to your computer and
read it in. For convenience, we will store it as an object named
`hotdog_cleaned`. You might need to change the file path to match your
setup.

<details>

<summary><b>Click to reveal code</b></summary>

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
hotdog_cleaned <- readr::read_csv("HotdogData.csv")
```

</details>

For our storytelling, we will want to add an image to the document. For
Closeread syntax, include `::::` before and after any insertions or
code. Below is the code to add the image from the sample Closeread. You
may use the same image or find your own and add it.

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
:::{#cr-intro}
![](https://www.tastingtable.com/img/gallery/everything-to-know-about-nathans-famous-july-4th-international-hot-dog-eating-contest-2024/intro-1719859047.jpg)
:::
```

Notice the `::::` before and after the code chunk. This is important in
Closeread syntax. Also notice `{#cr-intro}`. You will want to name each
addition to your document to be able to call them back later. We will go
into this further as we progress.

## Reshaping Data

After looking at some of the information on the [Wikipedia
page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nathan%27s_Hot_Dog_Eating_Contest){target="_blank"}
on the Nathan's Hotdog Eating Contest, you may have noticed that every
year has a male winner but the female winners are not as consistent.
Let's start by using the indicator variable for `Sex` and filtering to
only include male winners for our first visualization. For now we only
want to look at male winners.

Try on your own to use the `filter` function to modify the dataset.

<details>

<summary><b>Click to reveal code</b></summary>

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
hotdog_men <- hotdog_cleaned |> filter(Sex == 1)
```

## Creating Visualizations

Before we can move forward with the Closeread extension, we will want to
create the basic graph that we will work off of. Create a scatterplot
with Year on the x-axis and Hotdogs_per_minute on the y-axis. Add a
title and fix the labels. Here is the sample code if needed:

<details>

<summary><b>Click to reveal code</b></summary>

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
ggplot(data = hotdog_men, aes(x = Year, y = Hotdogs_per_minute)) +
  geom_point() +
  ylim(0, 8) + ## this line ensures the dimensions are the same for all plots
  labs(y = "Hotdogs Per Minute", 
       title = "Hotdogs Eaten by Competition Winner By Year") +
  theme_minimal()
```

</details>

## Starting with Closeread

There is a general format to all Closeread documents. Here is an example
to show you the syntax of adding `text sections that scroll` and
`stickies` that stay on the side. Note that stickies are named so they
can be called back with different sections of text without recoding each
time. We will use this specifically for our main graph in this module.

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
:::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-sticky-id}
Content that stays fixed (image, plot, text)
:::

Text that scrolls and triggers changes. @cr-sticky-id

:::
```

Once you have a general idea of how Closeread documents are coded and
how the syntax works we can begin to create a story.

Looking at the example Closeread document, we already have our heading
and main image added. We want some text to introduce our topic further.

Here is the text from the example, or create your own based off of what
you have read about.

<details>

<summary><b>Click to reveal code</b></summary>

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
::: cr-section
How many hotdogs per minute does the winner of each year's champion of the 
Nathan's Hotdog Eating Contest eat?
```

</details>

Note how the `:::` and `cr-section` are used.

#### Placing a graph as a sticky

We want to insert the graph we have made as a sticky on the right of the
document so that we can have associated scrolly text on the left. To do
so, you will want to name your graph with `::: {#cr-graphname}`. The
first graph in the example is named `@cr-biggraph`.

Uncomment any commented lines when you add to your document.

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
::: {#cr-biggraph}
## ```{r}
ggplot(data = hotdog_men, aes(x = Year, y = Hotdogs_per_minute)) +
  geom_point() +
  ylim(0, 8) +
  labs(y = "Hotdogs Per Minute",
       title = "Hotdogs Eaten by Competition Winner By Year") +
  theme_minimal()
##```
:::
```

Once you have your graph named as a sticky, it will not show up until
you call it. Add the text that you want to go along with your plot with
`@cr-biggraph` following your text to assign it to your plot.

Here is the example code, but feel free to write what you want.

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
There are a few interesting trends that stand out here. @cr-biggraph

Why do we think that there is a continual decrease in the first \~10 years?
```

Notice that any additional paragraphs of text do not need to call the
graph if they are directly following a paragraph that calls it. The
graph will not disappear unless you call something else or tell it to go
away.

### Runtime

We want to talk about the trend at the beginning of the graph now.
Looking at the data and reading about the contest from the Wikipedia
page, you will see that earlier competitions had much shorter runtimes
than more recent competitions.

Alter the original graph to highlight the first 8 years of the
competition and label the points with how many minutes the total
competition was.

<details>

<summary><b>Click to reveal code</b></summary>

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
## uncomment lines for r chunk
::: {#cr-runtime}
## ```{r echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

hotdog_time <- hotdog_men |> slice(44:51) 

ggplot(data = hotdog_men, aes(x = Year, y = Hotdogs_per_minute)) +
  geom_point() +
  ylim(0, 8) +
  labs(y = "Hotdogs Per Minute", title = "Hotdogs Eaten by Competition Winner By Year") +
  geom_text_repel(data = hotdog_time, aes(label = Time)) +
  geom_point(data = hotdog_time, size = 2, colour = "red") +
  theme_minimal()
## ```
:::
```

</details>

Add some text to explain the context of your graph to readers and call
the graph to go along with it.

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
This means that contestants were able to eat more hotdogs per minute as they 
were eating for less time. @cr-runtime
```

### 2001 Jump

We also want to explain the large jump in number of hotdogs eaten
between 2000 and 2001. If you have not already found the explanation for
this jump, continue reading the [Wikipedia
page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nathan%27s_Hot_Dog_Eating_Contest){target="_blank"}.

Once you have a general understanding to be able to continue your
storytelling, alter the original graph to highlight the winner in 2001
and label with the name of the winner. Use `geom_text_repel` to move the
name of the winner for aesthetic purposes. These coordinates have been
found to work best.

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
nudge_y = -0.75, nudge_x = -1
```

<details>

<summary><b>Click to reveal full code</b></summary>

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
hotdog_2001 <- hotdog_men |> slice(25) |>
  summarise(Year, Winner, Hotdogs_per_minute)

ggplot(data = hotdog_men, aes(x = Year, y = Hotdogs_per_minute)) +
  geom_point() +
  ylim(0, 8) +
  labs(y = "Hotdogs Per Minute", title = "Hotdogs Eaten by Competition Winner By Year") +
  geom_text_repel(data = hotdog_2001, aes(label = Winner),
                  nudge_y = -0.75, nudge_x = -1) +
  geom_point(data = hotdog_2001, size = 2, colour = "red") +
  theme_minimal()
```

</details>

Make sure to name the chunk appropriately and call it back with the
explanatory text that you want on the side.

<details>

<summary><b>Click to reveal text from example</b></summary>

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
There is also quite a large jump in number of hotdogs consumed per minute between
2000 and 2001. There were no changes in rules and both competitions were 12 
minutes. What could account for Kobayashi (2001 winner) eating nearly double the
amount that previous winners had? @cr-label2001
```

</details>

At this point in the example Closeread, there is an image included.
Either use the image provided or find your own to enhance your document.

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
::: {#cr-kobayashi}
![](https://i.guim.co.uk/img/media/c3fb7fa62b488a153448562ae34d77694a64299d/
      0_0_2849_2848/master/2849.jpg?width=480&dpr=1&s=none&crop=none)
:::

In 2001, Takeru Kobayashi introduced the Solomon method of competitive hotdog
eating. This includes breaking the hotdog in half, eating the meat first, and 
dipping the bun in water. @cr-kobayashi
```

### Kobayashi to Chestnut

As you have most likely learned from your reading or previous knowledge,
after Kobyashi reigned for many years, he was overtaken in 2007 by Joey
Chestnut.

Alter the original graph to make a visualization showing the years in
which Kobayashi won in one color and the years Chestnut won in another.
Label both with the name of the winner. Add a text section to go with
the plot.

<details>

<summary><b>Click to reveal code</b></summary>

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
hotdog_kobayashi <- hotdog_men |> filter(Winner == "Takeru Kobayashi")
hotdog_joey <- hotdog_men |> filter(Winner == "Joey Chestnut")
hotdog_kobayashi_label <- hotdog_kobayashi |> slice(4)
hotdog_joey_label <- hotdog_joey |> slice(15)

ggplot(data = hotdog_men, aes(x = Year, y = Hotdogs_per_minute)) +
  geom_point() +
  ylim(0, 8) +
  labs(y = "Hotdogs Per Minute", 
       title = "Hotdogs Eaten by Competition Winner By Year") +
  geom_text_repel(data = hotdog_kobayashi_label, aes(label = Winner), 
                  nudge_y = -0.5, colour = "red") +
  geom_point(data = hotdog_kobayashi, size = 2, colour = "red") +
  geom_text_repel(data = hotdog_joey_label, aes(label = Winner),
                  nudge_y = 0.5, colour = "dodgerblue") +
  geom_point(data = hotdog_joey, size = 2, colour = "dodgerblue") +
  theme_minimal()
```

</details>

<details>

<summary><b>Click to reveal text from example</b></summary>

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
Following the end to Kobayashi's reign, Joey Chestnut, the current name in 
Nathan hotdog competitive eating entered the scene. @cr-winnerswitch
```

</details>

### Chestnut's Wins

Referring back to the [Wikipedia
page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nathan%27s_Hot_Dog_Eating_Contest){target="_blank"},
add a section talking about the current champion and his wins (as well
as losses).

Alter either the original graph or the graph we just made to label only
Chestnut's wins and label his name.

<details>

<summary><b>Click to reveal code</b></summary>

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
hotdog_joey_label2 <- hotdog_joey |> slice(1)
ggplot(data = hotdog_men, aes(x = Year, y = Hotdogs_per_minute)) +
  geom_point() +
  ylim(0, 8) +
  labs(y = "Hotdogs Per Minute", title = "Hotdogs Eaten by Competition Winner By Year") +
  geom_text_repel(data = hotdog_joey_label2, aes(label = Winner),
                  nudge_x = 0.5, nudge_y = -0.4, colour = "dodgerblue") +
  geom_point(data = hotdog_joey_label2, size = 2, colour = "dodgerblue") +
  theme_minimal()
```

</details>

<details>

<summary><b>Click to reveal text from example</b></summary>

```{r echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
Joey Chestnut had a winning streak for years, excluding 2015 (when he lost with 
60 hotdogs compared to Matt Stonie's 62) and 2024 (when he was barred from the 
competition due to sponsership conflicts) @cr-joey
```

</details>

## Extensions for Further Storytelling

-   Continue the storytelling with a plot including data from female
    winners
    -   compare trends between male and female winners
    -   Miki Sudo reign
-   Find other stories from researching the competition to include in
    the storytelling
-   Create more Closeread documents with new data

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Optional: Instructor Materials for Introductory Statistics Courses" appearance="minimal"}
For Introductory Statistics Courses the data in the scatterplots can be assessed without the use of Closeread. Students are able to view the Closeread example to analyze the data and come to conclusions.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Conclusion" appearance="minimal"}
This module teaches the basics of how to use Closeread for data storytelling. After completing the module students should have a basic understanding of how to create their own Closeread story. It can be adapted for an introductory level class if needed. 
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Module Readings"}
-   [Nathan's Hotdog Eating Contest Wikipedia
    page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nathan%27s_Hot_Dog_Eating_Contest){target="_blank"}

    -   This page gives the background into the story that is being told through the Closeread. All information used in the example document as well as additional information can be found on this page. 


-   [Closeread website](https://closeread.dev){target="_blank"}

    -   The Closeread website provides information on what Closeread is, how it works, how to use it, and provides examples for newer learners.
  
-   [gastonsanchez.com](https://www.gastonsanchez.com/learn-closeread/modules/){target="_blank"}

    -  This website provides further information on creating Closeread documents and provides multiple examples for students to learn from.
:::
